analogy: Think of "Arts of the Indus Valley" through the chapter's own opening images: THE arts of the Indus Valley Civilisation emerged during the second half of the third millennium BCE. The forms of art found from various sites of the civilisation include sculptures, seals, pottery, jewellery, terracotta figures, etc. The artists of that time surely had fine artistic... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: ARTS OF, THE INDUS VALLEY, AN INTRODUCTION TO INDIAN ART 10, ARTS OF THE INDUS VALLEY 11. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Arts of the Indus Valley is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that tHE arts of the Indus Valley Civilisation emerged during the second half of the third millennium BCE.

The text explains that the forms of art found from various sites of the civilisation include sculptures, seals, pottery, jewellery, terracotta figures, etc.

The text explains that the artists of that time surely had fine artistic sensibilities and a vivid imagination.

The text explains that their delineation of human and animal figures was highly realistic in nature, since the anatomical details included in them were unique, and, in the case of terracotta art, the modelling of animal figures was done in an extremely careful manner.

The text explains that the two major sites of the Indus Valley Civilisation, along the Indus river—the cities of Harappa in the north and Mohenjodaro in the south—showcase one of earliest examples of civic planning.

The text explains that other markers were houses, markets, storage facilities, offices, public baths, etc., arranged in a grid-like pattern.

The text explains that there was also a highly developed drainage system.

The text explains that while Harappa and Mohenjodaro are situated in Pakistan, the important sites excavated in India are Lothal and Dholavira in Gujarat, Rakhigarhi in Haryana, Ropar in Punjab, Kalibangan in Rajasthan, etc.

The text explains that stone Statues Statues whether in stone, bronze or terracotta found in Harappan sites are not abundant, but refined.

The text explains that the stone statuaries found at Harappa and Mohenjodaro are excellent examples of handling three-dimensional volumes.

The text explains that in stone are two male figures—one is a torso in red sandstone and the other is a bust of a bearded man in soapstone—which are extensively discussed.

The text explains that the figure of the bearded man, interpreted as a priest, is draped in a shawl coming under the right arm and covering the left shoulder.

The text explains that this shawl is decorated with trefoil patterns.

The text explains that the eyes are a little elongated, and half-closed as in meditative concentration.

The text explains that the nose is well formed and of medium ARTS OF THE INDUS VALLEY Bust of a bearded priest 2

The text explains that aN INTRODUCTION TO INDIAN ART 10 size; the mouth is of average size with close-cut moustache and a short beard and whiskers; the ears resemble double shells with a hole in the middle.

The text explains that an armlet is worn on the right hand and holes around the neck suggest a necklace.

The text explains that in terracotta, we also find a few figurines of bearded males with coiled hair, their posture rigidly upright, legs slightly apart, and the arms parallel to the sides of the body.

The text explains that an elephant and a tiger are depicted to the right side of the seated figure, while on the left a rhinoceros and a buffalo are seen.

The text explains that the shawl covered the left shoulder passing below the right shoulder.

The text explains that the animal is shown standing with his head turned to the right and with a cord around the neck.

The text explains that bangles cover her left arm, a bracelet and an amulet or bangle adorn her right arm, and a cowry shell necklace is seen around her neck.

The text explains that her right hand is on her hip and her left hand is clasped in a traditional Indian dance gesture.

The text explains that the fan-shaped head-dress with a cup-like projection on each side is a distinct decorative feature of the mother goddess figures of the Indus Valley.

The text explains that pAINTED EARTHEN JAR Found in Mohenjodaro, this jar is made on a potter’s wheel with clay.

The text explains that the shape was manipulated by the pressure of the crafty fingers of the potter.

The text explains that mALE TORSO In this red sandstone figure, there are socket holes in the neck and shoulders for the attachment of head and arms.

The text explains that the frontal posture of the torso has been consciously adopted.

The text explains that the shoulders are well baked and the abdomen slightly prominent.

The text explains that after baking the clay model, it was painted with black colour.

The text explains that high polishing was done as a finishing touch.

The text explains that the motifs are of vegetals and geometric forms.

The text explains that designs are simple but with a tendency towards abstraction.

The text explains that map showing Mauryan sites (Outline map not to scale)

Source: NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art, Chapter 2: Arts of the Indus Valley

prelims_traps: Do not treat ARTS OF and THE INDUS VALLEY as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Arts of the Indus Valley; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Arts of the Indus Valley, which statement is supported by the source text about AN INTRODUCTION TO INDIAN ART 12?
options: Bronze Casting The art of bronze-casting was practised on a wide scale by the Harappans.
Their bronze statues were made using the ‘lost wax’ technique in which the wax figures were first covered with a coating of clay and...
Then the wax was heated and the molten wax was drained out through a tiny hole made in the clay cover.
The hollow mould thus created was filled with molten metal which took the original shape of the object.
